query: Franco-German nuclear steering group meeting 2026 Macron Merz
answer:
## Franco-German Nuclear Steering Group Meeting 2026: Macron & Merz

### Overview

On **March 2, 2026**, President Emmanuel Macron delivered what analysts call "the most consequential French nuclear speech in decades" from the **Île Longue submarine base** in Brittany. In it, he unveiled a new doctrine called **"dissuasion avancée"** (forward/advanced deterrence) and announced the creation of a **Franco-German nuclear steering group** — the institutional core of a broader European nuclear cooperation architecture.

---

### 1. The "Dissuasion Avancée" Doctrine

- Macron announced a **forward nuclear deterrence strategy** that would allow the temporary deployment of **nuclear-armed Rafale aircraft** to allied bases in extreme circumstances — though still under **sole French operational control**.
- **Eight countries initially signed up**: Germany, the United Kingdom, Poland, the Netherlands, Belgium, Greece, Sweden, and Denmark.
- **Norway joined as the ninth ally** in May 2026.
- France announced it would **increase its warhead count** above the current ~290 — the **first expansion since 1992** (when the arsenal peaked at ~540).
- France simultaneously announced it would **stop disclosing the arsenal's exact size**.
- Macron declared: *"To be free, one needs to be feared."*

### 2. The Franco-German Nuclear Steering Group

- The **Franco-German nuclear steering group** was established as the **bilateral institutional core** of the multilateral deterrence framework.
- Germany, under Chancellor **Friedrich Merz**, agreed to **participate conventionally in French nuclear exercises from 2026**.
- The steering group represents the most assertive German defense posture since reunification; Merz also aims to build "Europe's strongest conventional army."
- Germany's defense budget increased **25% to €82.69 billion (~$96 billion)**, with Merz targeting **3.5% NATO spending by 2029**.

### 3. Key Macron-Merz Interactions in 2026

| Date | Event |
|---|---|
| **March 2, 2026** | Macron's Île Longue speech; Franco-German nuclear steering group announced |
| **March 19–20, 2026** | EU Council summit in Brussels (both attended; divergences on Iran emerged) |
| **April 22, 2026** | Both leaders attended a meeting at the Permanent Joint Headquarters at Northwood (UK) to plan a multinational mine-clearance and maritime protection mission |

### 4. Political Constraints & Risks

- **Marine Le Pen's National Rally (RN) opposes sharing France's nuclear deterrence**, creating a hard deadline around the **April 2027 French presidential election** (13 months from announcement).
- Analysts note Macron is deliberately trying to **lock in institutional arrangements** before 2027, building "an architecture he hopes will survive him."
- A Franco-German rift on **Iran policy** (Merz called the Iranian regime "terrorist," breaking alignment with UK/France) raised questions about the durability of the partnership.
- Russia's Foreign Minister **Lavrov publicly criticized** the Macron-Merz nuclear alignment.

### 5. Analyst Assessment

The initiative has been described as *"Europe's most consequential security shift since the Cold War"* — moving toward a European nuclear architecture that exists **outside NATO's command structure**. However, the binding constraint is whether the framework can be institutionally locked in before the April 2027 French election.

---

sources.snippet: A Franco-German nuclear steering group was established with Germany to participate conventionally in French nuclear exercises from 2026. The Franco-German steering group makes it bilateral.

sources.title: Nuclear weapons and France - Wikipedia
sources.snippet: On 2 March 2026, President Emmanuel Macron announced that France had initiated talks with eight European allies in a scheme of cooperation he called 'dissuasion avancée'. He also announced an increase in the size of the French nuclear stockpile.

Germany under Chancellor Friedrich Merz agreed to participate conventionally in French nuclear exercises from 2026.
Eight countries initially signed up: Germany, UK, Poland, Netherlands, Belgium, Greece, Sweden, and Denmark. Norway joined as the ninth ally in May 2026.
France announced it would increase its warhead count above ~290 — the first expansion since 1992 — while ceasing to disclose the exact arsenal size.
The doctrine allows temporary deployment of nuclear-armed Rafale aircraft to allied bases in extreme circumstances, under sole French operational control.
Marine Le Pen's National Rally opposes nuclear deterrence sharing, creating a hard political deadline at the April 2027 French presidential election.
Macron and Merz met at the EU Council summit (March 19-20, 2026) and at the Northwood HQ meeting (April 22, 2026) on related defense matters.
Germany's defense budget increased 25% to €82.69 billion (~$96 billion); Merz targets 3.5% NATO spending by 2029.
